title: BPF & eBPF Deep Dive
description: BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter), evolved into eBPF (extended BPF), is a revolutionary kernel technology that allows running sandboxed programs in the kernel without changing kernel code or loading modules. eBPF enables safe, efficient, and programmable observability, networking, and security. At FAANG, eBPF powers critical infrastructure: Cilium (Kubernetes networking), Falco (security), Pixie (observability), and custom monitoring tools.

sections.title: eBPF Architecture & Concepts
sections.description: eBPF programs are written in a restricted C, compiled to BPF bytecode, verified by the kernel (safety checks: no loops with unbounded iterations, valid memory access, max instruction count), and attached to events (syscalls, tracepoints, kprobes, network interfaces). The in-kernel verifier is what makes eBPF safe — it rejects any program that could crash or hang the kernel. At FAANG, eBPF is used for: performance monitoring (bcc/bpftrace tools), network observability (Cilium), security auditing (Falco), and custom container monitoring.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Check eBPF support ===
cat /proc/sys/kernel/unprivileged_bpf_disabled  # 0 = enabled, 1 = disabled
# Check if BPF syscall is available:
grep bpf /proc/kallsyms | head -5

# === eBPF program types ===
# Kprobes     — Dynamic kernel function tracing
# Tracepoints — Static kernel tracepoints
# Perf events — PMC-based sampling
# XDP         — eXpress Data Path (network driver level)
# TC          — Traffic Control (network stack level)
# cgroup      — cgroup operations (device, socket, sysctl)

# === The eBPF lifecycle ===
# 1. Write BPF program (C / Rust / Python / Lua)
# 2. Compile to BPF bytecode (LLVM/clang)
# 3. Load into kernel (bpf() syscall)
# 4. Kernel verifier checks safety
# 5. JIT compiler translates to native code
# 6. Attach to event (kprobe, tracepoint, etc.)
# 7. Program runs on event, writes to BPF maps
# 8. User-space reads maps via bpf() syscall

# === BPF maps (data structures) ===
# Hash maps, arrays, per-CPU arrays, stacks, queues, ring buffers
# Shared between kernel BPF program and user-space
# Used for: aggregated metrics, configuration, state

# === Limitations ===
# - Max 4096 instructions (can be extended to 1M with tail calls)
# - No loops until recently (bounded loops added in Linux 5.17)
# - No kernel function calls (must be inlined) until BPF helpers
# - Stack limited to 512 bytes

sections.title: bpftrace: High-Level eBPF Tracing
sections.description: bpftrace is a high-level tracing language for Linux eBPF. It's like awk for kernel tracing — you write one-liners to observe kernel and user-space events. At FAANG, bpftrace is the go-to tool for ad-hoc performance investigations and root cause analysis.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Install bpftrace ===
sudo apt install bpftrace

# === File open tracing ===
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_openat { printf("%s %s\n", comm, str(args->filename)); }'
# Shows every file opened, by which process

# === New process tracing ===
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_execve { printf("%s -> %s\n", comm, str(args->filename)); }'

# === Kill tracing (who is sending SIGKILL?) ===
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_kill { printf("%s -> PID %d SIG %d\n", comm, args->pid, args->sig); }'

# === OOM tracing ===
bpftrace -e 'kprobe:oom_kill_process { printf("OOM killed %s (%d)\n", str(args->task->comm), args->task->pid); }'

# === Count syscalls by process ===
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_* { @[comm] = count(); }'

# === Block I/O tracing ===
bpftrace -e 'kprobe:blk_account_io_start { printf("I/O: %s %s\n", comm, args->rwbs); }'

# === TCP connection tracing ===
bpftrace -e 'kprobe:tcp_connect { printf("%s connected to %s:%d\n", comm, ntop(args->sk->__sk_common->skc_daddr), args->sk->__sk_common->skc_dport); }'

# === One-shot latency histogram (read syscall) ===
bpftrace -e 'kprobe:vfs_read { @start[tid] = nsecs; } kretprobe:vfs_read /@start[tid]/ { @us = hist((nsecs - @start[tid]) / 1000); delete(@start[tid]); }'

# === Profile: CPU sampling ===
bpftrace -e 'profile:hz:99 { @[kstack, comm] = count(); }'  # Kernel stack
bpftrace -e 'profile:hz:99 { @[ustack, comm] = count(); }'  # User stack

# === Built-in tools ===
sudo bpftrace -l                     # List all probes
sudo bpftrace -l 'tracepoint:syscalls:*'  # List syscall tracepoints
# Many pre-built tools come with bpftrace:
ls /usr/share/bpftrace/tools/
# opensnoop.bt, killsnoop.bt, execsnoop.bt, tcplife.bt, ...

# Run a pre-built tool:
sudo bpftrace /usr/share/bpftrace/tools/opensnoop.bt

sections.title: bcc: The BPF Compiler Collection
sections.description: bcc (BPF Compiler Collection) provides Python- and Lua-based tools for eBPF tracing. It's more flexible than bpftrace (full programming language support) but also more complex. bcc tools are production-ready — many are used daily at FAANG for performance monitoring.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Install bcc ===
sudo apt install bpfcc-tools   # Ubuntu package name
# Tools are prefixed with -bpfcc:

# === Essential bcc tools ===
# opensnoop: trace file opens
sudo opensnoop-bpfcc

# execsnoop: trace new processes
sudo execsnoop-bpfcc

# biotop: block I/O by process
sudo biotop-bpfcc

# tcplife: TCP connection lifecycle
sudo tcplife-bpfcc

# tcptop: TCP throughput by process
sudo tcptop-bpfcc

# cpuunclaimed: CPU not claimed by scheduler
sudo cpuunclaimed-bpfcc

# offcputime: trace blocked time (off-CPU analysis)
sudo offcputime-bpfcc -K 30

# runqlat: scheduler run-queue latency
sudo runqlat-bpfcc

# profile: CPU sampling with frames
sudo profile-bpfcc -af 30

# cachestat: page cache hit rate
sudo cachestat-bpfcc

# llcstat: Last-level cache misses
sudo llcstat-bpfcc

# === Custom bcc Python script (minimal example) ===
cat > trace_open.py << 'EOF'
from bcc import BPF

# BPF C program
bpf_program = """
int kprobe__do_sys_openat2(struct pt_regs *ctx, int dfd, const char __user *filename, int flags) {
    bpf_trace_printk("openat: %s\\n", filename);
    return 0;
}
"""

b = BPF(text=bpf_program)
b.trace_print()
EOF

sudo python3 trace_open.py

sections.title: XDP & eBPF Networking
sections.description: XDP (eXpress Data Path) is an eBPF-based packet processing framework that runs at the network driver level — before the kernel's network stack processes the packet. This enables DDoS mitigation, load balancing, and packet filtering at line rate (millions of packets per second). At FAANG, XDP is used for: DDoS protection (Cloudflare), Kubernetes service load balancing (Cilium), and custom network filtering.
sections.codeBlock:
# === XDP program flow ===
# 1. Packet arrives at NIC
# 2. XDP program runs (driver level, before skb allocation)
# 3. Actions: XDP_DROP, XDP_PASS, XDP_TX, XDP_REDIRECT
# 4. Result: drop packet (fast), pass to kernel stack (normal),
#            transmit back out (hairpin), or redirect to another NIC

# === XDP vs kernel bypass (DPDK) ===
# XDP: kernel is still in control, safe, programmable, no userspace changes
# DPDK: bypasses kernel entirely, userspace driver, fastest possible,
#       but requires dedicated CPUs and application changes

# === Cilium (Kubernetes CNI powered by eBPF) ===
# Cilium uses eBPF for:
# - Service load balancing (replacing kube-proxy)
# - Network policies (L3/L4/L7)
# - Observability (Hubble)
# - Transparent encryption

# === Check if XDP is supported ===
ip link show eth0                     # Check features: xdp
# Enable XDP on interface:
sudo ip link set dev eth0 xdp obj my_xdp_prog.o sec xdp

# === XDP example: DDoS blacklist ===
# Simple XDP program to drop packets from blacklisted IPs
# (Conceptual — real XDP uses BPF maps for dynamic blacklists)

# === eBPF for security: Falco ===
# Falco uses eBPF tracepoints/kprobes to detect:
# - Container escape attempts
# - Unauthorized file access
# - Suspicious network connections
# - Privilege escalation

# Install Falco (using eBPF probe):
# falco driver-loader bpf
# falco --bpf

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'How does eBPF differ from a kernel module?'

A: 'A kernel module can access any kernel function, data structure, or memory — it can crash the entire system. eBPF programs are verified by the kernel's BPF verifier before loading — the verifier checks for: (1) valid memory access (no null pointers), (2) bounded loops (termination guaranteed), (3) maximum instruction count (4096 default, up to 1M with tail calls), (4) valid helper function calls. If verification fails, the program is rejected. This safety guarantee is what makes eBPF deployable in production — a buggy eBPF program can't crash the kernel like a buggy module can.'

Q: 'What's a real-world use case for eBPF at FAANG?'

A: 'One example: Cilium at scale. At a FAANG company using Kubernetes with 10,000+ nodes, Cilium uses eBPF to replace kube-proxy for service load balancing — instead of iptables rules (which become O(n) with number of services), eBPF programs are updated per-service and run at the XDP or TC layer. This eliminates the iptables scaling bottleneck. Another example: Datadog's continuous profiler uses eBPF to sample stack traces without any application changes — no agent code, no recompilation, no JVM flags.'

Q: 'What are the limitations of eBPF?'

A: '(1) Instruction limit: 4096 by default (can be extended). (2) Stack limit: 512 bytes — you can't allocate large local data structures. (3) No unbounded loops (before Linux 5.17). (4) Limited helper functions — you can call only a fixed set of BPF helpers, not arbitrary kernel functions. (5) Verifier complexity — the verifier can be slow for complex programs. (6) No floating-point arithmetic. (7) Kernel version dependence — newer BPF features require newer kernels. At FAANG, we run custom kernels (or at least Linux 5.10+) specifically to get modern eBPF features.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• What is eBPF? — Official eBPF foundation introduction
  • bcc GitHub — Tools and examples for BPF Compiler Collection
  • bpftrace GitHub — One-liners, tools, and documentation
  • Cilium — Kubernetes CNI powered by eBPF

details:
FAANG Architect's Note: eBPF is the most important Linux kernel technology of the last decade. It's transforming observability, networking, and security — without eBPF, you'd need kernel modules (dangerous) or ptrace (slow) for most of these use cases. At FAANG, eBPF is not a 'nice to have' — it's infrastructure. Cilium, Falco, Pixie, and Datadog's continuous profiler all rely on eBPF. If you're working on infrastructure at FAANG, learning eBPF (even just the tooling like bpftrace and bcc) will give you debugging superpowers that traditional tools can't match.

Production Tip: bpftrace is safe for production use — it's designed to never crash the system. However, certain bcc tools (especially those using kprobes at high frequency) can add measurable overhead. Start with the pre-built tools (execsnoop, opensnoop, etc.) — they're optimized for low overhead. For custom BPF programs, test in a staging environment first and always monitor CPU usage of the BPF program itself (visible via bpftool prog show).
